title: Standard UI Components & Design Best Practices
description: Catalog of common front-end UI components with anatomy, UX and accessibility best practices, and defining traits.

entries.name: Button (primary / secondary / ghost / destructive)
entries.summary: A clickable control that triggers an action or event. Variants signal hierarchy: primary (filled, one per view), secondary (outlined/tonal), ghost/text (low emphasis), and destructive (danger red).
entries.significance: The most fundamental interactive control; button hierarchy is how an interface communicates the single most important next action versus supporting ones.
key_facts: Touch target should be at least 44x44 pt (Apple HIG) or 48x48 dp (Material); WCAG 2.5.8 AA requires 24x24 CSS px minimum.
Use one primary (high-emphasis) button per view to keep visual hierarchy clear; overusing primary buttons flattens hierarchy.
Button label should be a verb describing the outcome ('Save changes', 'Delete account'), not generic 'OK'/'Submit' where avoidable.
Must have a visible focus state (focus-visible outline) and support activation via both Enter and Space keys per ARIA button pattern.
Destructive actions should use color plus text/icon (not color alone) and often a confirmation step to prevent accidental irreversible loss.

entries.name: Icon button
entries.summary: A button whose only visible content is an icon, used for compact or repeated actions such as toolbar controls, close (x), or favorite (heart).
entries.significance: Saves space in dense toolbars and mobile bars, but is the single most common source of unlabeled controls that break screen-reader access.
key_facts: Requires an accessible name via aria-label or visually-hidden text because there is no visible label; icon alone is not announced meaningfully.
The interactive hit area should remain at least 44x44 pt / 48x48 dp even when the icon glyph is small (e.g. 24px).
Ambiguous icons (icon-only) benefit from a tooltip on hover/focus to disclose meaning.
Toggle icon buttons (e.g. mute/unmute) should expose state with aria-pressed.

entries.name: Floating Action Button (FAB)
entries.summary: A circular, elevated button that floats above content in a fixed position to represent the primary, most common action of a screen (e.g. compose, add).
entries.significance: A signature Material Design pattern that promotes a single screen-level action to persistent, thumb-reachable prominence on mobile.
key_facts: Represents one primary action per screen; multiple competing FABs dilute its purpose.
Standard Material FAB is 56dp; small FAB 40dp; large FAB 96dp, keeping it within the 48dp minimum touch guidance.
Should not obscure other controls, bottom navigation, or important content beneath it.
Because it is fixed-position, ensure it does not overlap content or the on-screen keyboard on small viewports.

entries.name: Text input (text field)
entries.summary: A single-line field for entering short free-form text such as a name, email, or search term.
entries.significance: The workhorse of forms; small details of labeling and validation drive most form-completion success or failure.
key_facts: Every field needs a persistent visible label programmatically associated via <label for> or aria-labelledby; placeholder text is not a substitute (it disappears on input).
Use the correct input type/inputmode (email, tel, url, number) so mobile keyboards and validation adapt.
Show validation errors inline next to the field, with an icon plus text (not color alone) for colorblind users.
Do not disable copy/paste; support browser autofill via correct autocomplete attributes.

sources.title: 10 Design Guidelines for Reporting Errors in Forms

entries.name: Textarea (multi-line input)
entries.summary: A resizable, multi-line text field for longer free-form content such as comments, messages, or descriptions.
entries.significance: Signals to users that a longer response is expected; sizing communicates the anticipated answer length.
key_facts: Initial height should hint at expected input length; auto-grow avoids scrollbars for long content.
Requires an associated visible label like any input.
Provide a character counter when a max length applies, and announce remaining count to assistive tech.
Preserve the user's resize handle where the platform supports it rather than locking dimensions.

entries.name: Select / Dropdown
entries.summary: A control that lets a user choose one (or several) options from a predefined list revealed in a popup.
entries.significance: Efficient for many mutually-exclusive options, but native selects and custom listboxes differ sharply in accessibility and styling tradeoffs.
key_facts: Prefer the native <select> where possible for built-in keyboard, mobile, and screen-reader support; custom widgets must implement the ARIA listbox pattern.
For fewer than ~5 mutually exclusive options, radio buttons are often more usable than a dropdown because all choices are visible.
Provide a clear label and, when required, a sensible or empty default rather than a pre-selected value the user might miss.
Custom dropdowns must support arrow-key navigation, type-ahead, Home/End, and Escape to close.

entries.name: Combobox / Autocomplete
entries.summary: A text input paired with a filtered popup of suggestions, letting users type to narrow a large set of options.
entries.significance: Bridges free text and constrained choice; essential when the option set is too large for a plain dropdown (cities, tags, users).
key_facts: Implements the ARIA combobox pattern: the input has role combobox with aria-expanded and aria-controls pointing to the popup.
Suggestions should update as the user types and be navigable with arrow keys; Escape closes the popup.
The popup may be a listbox, grid, tree, or dialog per the APG combobox definition.
Announce the number of results and the active option to screen readers via aria-activedescendant.

entries.name: Checkbox
entries.summary: A control for independent on/off choices, allowing zero, one, or many selections within a group.
entries.significance: The standard for multi-select and single boolean opt-ins (terms acceptance); its independence distinguishes it from radios.
key_facts: Use checkboxes when options are independent and multiple can be selected; use radios for mutually exclusive choice.
The label must be clickable and associated with the box; clicking the text should toggle the state.
Supports three visual states where needed: unchecked, checked, and indeterminate (partial group selection).
Follows the ARIA checkbox pattern: toggled with Space, exposes aria-checked including 'mixed'.

entries.name: Radio button (radio group)
entries.summary: A set of buttons where selecting one deselects the others, used for mutually exclusive choices from a small visible list.
entries.significance: Makes all options visible at once, reducing interaction cost versus a dropdown for short option sets.
key_facts: Radios belong to a named group; only one may be selected at a time.
Per the ARIA radiogroup pattern, arrow keys move selection within the group and the group is a single Tab stop (roving tabindex).
Best for 2-5 mutually exclusive options; beyond that consider a select/dropdown.
Avoid pre-selecting a default when a deliberate choice matters (e.g. sensitive settings).

entries.name: Toggle / Switch
entries.summary: A control that flips a single setting immediately on or off, mimicking a physical light switch.
entries.significance: Signals an instant state change (no Save needed), distinguishing it from a checkbox that typically commits with form submission.
key_facts: Use a switch for settings that take effect immediately; use a checkbox when the choice is submitted as part of a form.
Follows the ARIA switch pattern with role switch and aria-checked true/false, toggled by Space/Enter.
State must be conveyed by more than thumb position/color, e.g. an on/off label or icon, for colorblind users.
The whole track/thumb plus label should meet the minimum touch target size.

entries.name: Slider (range)
entries.summary: A control for selecting a value (or range) from a continuous or stepped scale by dragging a thumb along a track.
entries.significance: Good for approximate, visual value setting (volume, brightness, price range) where exact precision is secondary.
key_facts: Follows the ARIA slider pattern: arrow keys adjust by step, Home/End jump to min/max, and it exposes aria-valuenow/min/max.
Provide a visible current value and, when precision matters, pair with a numeric input.
The draggable thumb must meet touch-target size even if the visual dot is small.
Range (two-thumb) sliders should prevent thumbs from crossing and label each thumb.

entries.name: Stepper / Number spinner
entries.summary: A numeric field with increment and decrement buttons for adjusting a value in discrete steps (e.g. quantity).
entries.significance: Best for small numeric adjustments near a known value; direct typing plus buttons covers both fine and coarse changes.
key_facts: Follows the ARIA spinbutton pattern with aria-valuenow/min/max; Up/Down arrows step the value.
The +/- buttons must each meet minimum touch target size and be spaced to avoid mis-taps.
Allow direct keyboard entry for large jumps rather than forcing many button presses.
Clamp to min/max and give feedback when a limit is reached.

entries.name: Date picker
entries.summary: A control for selecting a date (or range) via a calendar grid, often paired with a typed date field.
entries.significance: One of the hardest components to get right for accessibility and localization; date format ambiguity causes frequent user errors.
key_facts: Offer both a typeable date field and a calendar picker; typing is faster for known dates, the calendar helps for relative ones.
The calendar grid should use a proper grid/table structure with arrow-key navigation between days and clear focus.
Show the date format expected (e.g. DD/MM/YYYY) to avoid ambiguity; respect locale.
Disable and announce unavailable dates rather than allowing invalid selection.

entries.name: File upload
entries.summary: A control for selecting files from the device, often supporting drag-and-drop plus a fallback file-browse button.
entries.significance: Involves error-prone edge cases (size limits, types, progress, failure) where feedback quality determines success.
key_facts: Always provide a clickable button fallback in addition to any drag-and-drop zone; drag-and-drop alone is inaccessible.
State accepted file types and size limits up front, and validate with a clear error on violation.
Show upload progress and allow cancel/retry; confirm success and let users remove added files.
The underlying <input type=file> must have an associated label for screen readers.

entries.name: Search field
entries.summary: A specialized text input for entering queries, typically with a magnifier icon, clear (x) button, and often suggestions.
entries.significance: A primary navigation path on content-heavy sites; discoverability and forgiving matching drive its usefulness.
key_facts: Use input type=search and a labeled control (role search landmark for the region) so assistive tech identifies it.
Provide a clear/reset (x) button once text is entered.
Suggestions/autocomplete should follow the combobox pattern and be keyboard navigable.
Keep the field visible (not hidden behind an icon) on content sites where search is heavily used.

entries.name: Form & validation
entries.summary: The composition and validation of grouped inputs, including error reporting, required-field marking, and submission handling.
entries.significance: Forms are where users most often fail; how errors are surfaced and worded is decisive for completion rates.
key_facts: Report errors inline next to the offending field and keep the message visible while the user fixes it, minimizing memory load.
Use icon plus text (not color alone) so errors are perceivable by colorblind users; associate the message via aria-describedby.
Validate on blur / after a field is completed for format checks; avoid interrupting mid-typing.
On submit, move focus to the first error or an error summary and use aria-live to announce it.

sources.title: 10 Design Guidelines for Reporting Errors in Forms

entries.name: Card
entries.summary: A self-contained surface grouping related content and actions about a single subject, often arranged in grids or lists.
entries.significance: A flexible container that packages heterogeneous content into scannable, tappable units; central to feed and dashboard layouts.
key_facts: A card should represent a single coherent subject; mixing unrelated content weakens scannability.
If the whole card is clickable, ensure one clear primary link and avoid nested interactive elements that trap keyboard/screen-reader users.
Maintain consistent internal padding and a clear content order (media, title, supporting text, actions).
Elevation/shadow or border should distinguish the card surface from the background for sufficient contrast.

entries.name: Modal / Dialog
entries.summary: A window overlaid on the page that requires interaction before returning to the main content, which is inert while it is open.
entries.significance: A heavyweight interruption appropriate only when the user truly must act (destructive confirmation, blocking task); overuse harms flow.
key_facts: Content behind a modal is inert; focus must be trapped inside and returned to the trigger on close per the APG dialog pattern.
Escape closes the dialog; the dialog uses role dialog with aria-modal and an accessible name via aria-labelledby.
Modals interrupt and demand action; reserve them for consequential or destructive decisions (NN/G).
On open, move focus into the dialog (first field or the dialog itself), not left on the background.

sources.title: Modal & Nonmodal Dialogs: When (& When Not) to Use Them

entries.name: Drawer / Sheet
entries.summary: A panel that slides in from a screen edge (side drawer) or bottom (bottom sheet) to reveal navigation or contextual content.
entries.significance: Provides on-demand access to secondary content or navigation without leaving the current context, especially on mobile.
key_facts: Modal drawers dim and block background and need focus trapping; standard/persistent drawers coexist with content.
Bottom sheets are thumb-reachable on mobile and should be dismissable by swipe-down and by an explicit control.
Provide a visible affordance (drag handle) and an accessible close control, not gesture-only dismissal.
Ensure the drawer's scrim and content meet contrast requirements over the underlying page.

sources.title: Bottom Sheets: Definition and UX Guidelines

entries.name: Popover
entries.summary: A small non-modal overlay anchored to a trigger element that shows contextual content or actions, dismissed by clicking away.
entries.significance: Delivers contextual controls/details without a full modal interruption; lighter weight than a dialog.
key_facts: Unlike a modal, a popover is non-modal: the rest of the page stays interactive, and it dismisses on outside click or Escape.
It should be positioned relative to its trigger and reposition to stay within the viewport (flip/shift).
Manage focus: move focus in for interactive popovers and return it to the trigger on close.
Distinguish from tooltip (informational, hover) and menu (list of commands).

entries.name: Tooltip
entries.summary: A small transient label that appears on hover or focus to describe an element, typically an icon-only control.
entries.significance: Discloses meaning of ambiguous controls, but is one of the most misused patterns for essential or interactive content.
key_facts: Tooltips must be reachable by keyboard focus, not hover-only, per the ARIA tooltip pattern.
Never put essential information or interactive elements inside a tooltip; it is supplementary and transient.
Associate it with aria-describedby so screen readers announce it; Escape should dismiss it (WCAG 1.4.13).
Keep it brief (a few words); use a popover for richer content.
examples: Truncated text full value on hover

entries.name: Toast / Snackbar
entries.summary: A brief, auto-dismissing message that confirms an action or reports a lightweight status without interrupting the task.
entries.significance: The standard non-blocking confirmation pattern; timing and persistence tradeoffs affect whether users perceive them.
key_facts: Announce toasts through an aria-live region (polite) so screen-reader users are notified without focus change.
Keep messages short; if the toast contains an action (Undo), give enough time (Material suggests a few seconds) and a way to pause/dismiss.
Do not use auto-dismissing toasts for critical errors the user must act on; use a persistent alert instead.
Position consistently (commonly bottom on mobile, corner on desktop) and avoid stacking many at once.

entries.name: Alert / Banner
entries.summary: A prominent, persistent message conveying important status (error, warning, success, info) inline within the page.
entries.significance: Communicates conditions users must notice or act on; persistence distinguishes it from transient toasts.
key_facts: Use role alert for time-sensitive messages so assistive tech announces them immediately; role status for less urgent updates.
Convey severity with an icon and text, not color alone, and use accessible color contrast.
Page-level banners (e.g. cookie/system notices) should be dismissable and not trap focus.
Reserve error styling for genuine errors; overusing red desensitizes users.

entries.name: Badge
entries.summary: A small numeric or dot indicator overlaid on an element to show a count (unread) or status.
entries.significance: Draws attention to changed or pending state at a glance; a compact notification cue.
key_facts: Numeric badges should cap large counts (e.g. '99+') to preserve layout.
Because a badge conveys meaning, expose it to screen readers (e.g. 'Inbox, 3 unread'), not just visually.
A dot badge indicates 'something new' without a count; ensure it is perceivable beyond color alone.
Keep badges small and high-contrast against their anchor icon.

entries.name: Chip / Tag
entries.summary: A compact element representing an input, attribute, filter, or selection, often removable or toggleable.
entries.significance: Encapsulates discrete items (filters, recipients, tags) into scannable, manipulable tokens.
key_facts: Chip types differ: input/token chips (removable), filter chips (toggle selection), choice/assist chips.
Removable chips need an accessible remove control with a label (e.g. 'Remove tag: design') meeting touch-target size.
Filter chips should expose selected state to assistive tech (aria-pressed or as part of a listbox).
Keep chip labels short; truncate with a tooltip for overflow.

entries.name: Avatar
entries.summary: A small graphic representing a person or entity: a photo, initials, or icon fallback.
entries.significance: Provides quick visual identity in lists, comments, and headers; personalizes and speeds recognition.
key_facts: Provide a text fallback (initials or generic icon) when no image exists, and meaningful alt text for the person's name.
Decorative avatars adjacent to a visible name can use empty alt to avoid duplicate announcement.
Avatar groups/stacks should indicate overflow (e.g. '+5') accessibly.
Maintain consistent shape (circle/square) and size scale across the system.

entries.name: Accordion
entries.summary: A vertically stacked set of headers that expand and collapse to reveal or hide their associated content sections.
entries.significance: Manages long content by progressive disclosure, reducing scrolling; common for FAQs and settings.
key_facts: Each header is a button with aria-expanded and aria-controls pointing to its panel, per the APG accordion pattern.
Enter/Space toggles a section; the header button should span the full clickable width.
Decide whether multiple panels can be open at once; single-open behavior should be predictable.
Avoid hiding content users need to compare across sections or that should be indexable/printed.

entries.name: Tabs
entries.summary: A set of layered content panels where a row of tabs lets users switch which single panel is visible.
entries.significance: Organizes peer content into one region, saving space; effective when only one section is needed at a time.
key_facts: Follows the APG tabs pattern: tablist with roving tabindex, arrow keys move between tabs, each tab controls a tabpanel.
The selected tab uses aria-selected; its panel is associated via aria-controls / aria-labelledby.
Keep tab labels short and equally-weighted (peer content); do not use tabs for sequential steps (use a wizard).
Avoid too many tabs that wrap or scroll off-screen; consider a menu or different structure instead.

entries.name: Breadcrumb
entries.summary: A horizontal trail showing the current page's location within the site hierarchy, with links back to ancestor levels.
entries.significance: Aids wayfinding in deep hierarchies and provides quick one-click ascent without replacing primary navigation.
key_facts: Wrap in a nav element with an accessible name (e.g. aria-label='Breadcrumb') per the APG breadcrumb pattern.
The current page is the last item and marked with aria-current='page' (usually non-linked).
Separators (>, /) should be decorative/CSS, not read as content by screen readers.
Best for hierarchical sites with depth; unnecessary on flat structures.

entries.name: Pagination
entries.summary: Controls that split a large result set into discrete numbered pages with previous/next navigation.
entries.significance: Gives users control and a sense of scope over long lists, and supports returning to a known position, versus infinite scroll.
key_facts: Wrap in a nav landmark with an accessible name; mark the active page with aria-current='page'.
Prev/Next controls need discernible labels and a disabled state at the boundaries.
Page-number targets must meet minimum touch-target size and spacing.
Prefer pagination over infinite scroll when users need to find, bookmark, or return to specific items or reach a footer.

entries.name: Navbar (top navigation)
entries.summary: A persistent horizontal bar, usually at the top, containing primary navigation, branding, and global actions.
entries.significance: The primary orientation and navigation anchor of most sites and apps; its clarity shapes the whole information architecture.
key_facts: Use a nav landmark; the current section should be indicated with aria-current='page'.
On small screens it commonly collapses into a menu button ('hamburger') that must be labeled and keyboard operable.
Keep top-level items few and stable; prioritize the most important destinations.
Ensure the whole bar and its controls are keyboard-navigable in a logical order and have visible focus.

entries.name: Sidebar / Navigation rail
entries.summary: A vertical navigation panel along the screen edge; a full-width sidebar (with labels) or a compact icon-only rail.
entries.significance: Standard for app-like layouts with many destinations; the rail form conserves space while keeping navigation persistent.
key_facts: Use a nav landmark and indicate the active destination with aria-current.
Icon-only rails need accessible labels (aria-label or tooltip) for each item.
Collapsible sidebars should remember state and provide an accessible toggle.
Each destination target should meet touch-target size, especially in compact rails.

entries.name: Menu (menu button)
entries.summary: A popup list of commands or links revealed by a trigger (menu button) or right-click (context menu).
entries.significance: Consolidates a set of actions into an on-demand list, keeping the interface uncluttered.
key_facts: Follows the APG menu button pattern: the trigger has aria-haspopup and aria-expanded; the popup uses role menu with menuitem children.
Arrow keys move between items, Enter/Space activates, Escape closes and returns focus to the trigger.
A menu is for actions/commands; a listbox/select is for choosing a value, do not conflate them.
Keep menus shallow; deep nested submenus are hard to operate, especially on touch.

entries.name: Command palette
entries.summary: A keyboard-invoked overlay (often Cmd/Ctrl+K) combining search with a filtered list of commands and destinations.
entries.significance: A power-user pattern that turns navigation and actions into fast, type-to-find interactions; popularized by developer tools.
key_facts: Combines the modal dialog and combobox patterns: a focus-trapped overlay containing a filterable command input.
Typically invoked with Cmd+K / Ctrl+K; arrow keys navigate results, Enter executes, Escape closes.
Results should be fuzzy-matched and ranked, showing keyboard shortcuts where they exist.
Must remain accessible: announce result counts and the active option via combobox semantics.

entries.name: Table / Data grid
entries.summary: A grid of rows and columns for displaying and comparing structured data, often with sorting, filtering, and selection.
entries.significance: The primary way to present dense structured data for scanning and comparison; accessibility and responsive behavior are notoriously tricky.
key_facts: Use semantic table markup (th with scope) for static data; interactive editable grids follow the APG grid pattern with arrow-key cell navigation.
Header cells must be associated with their columns/rows so screen readers announce context per cell.
Sortable columns expose sort state via aria-sort; row selection controls need accessible labels.
On small screens, reflow or prioritize columns rather than forcing horizontal scroll of everything.

entries.name: List
entries.summary: A vertical sequence of related items, each often combining an icon/avatar, primary and secondary text, and trailing controls.
entries.significance: The most common way to present a collection of homogeneous items for scanning and selection.
key_facts: Use semantic list markup (ul/ol/li) so assistive tech announces item count and position.
Keep a consistent row structure so users can scan; align leading and trailing elements.
Interactive list items need a clear primary action and adequate row height for touch targets.
For very long lists, use virtualization for performance while preserving keyboard access and semantics.

entries.name: Progress bar
entries.summary: A horizontal indicator showing how far a determinate task has advanced, or indeterminate activity when the end is unknown.
entries.significance: Reduces perceived wait and uncertainty by showing that work is happening and, ideally, how much remains.
key_facts: Use role progressbar with aria-valuenow/min/max for determinate progress so assistive tech reads the percentage.
Use a determinate bar when completion can be estimated; use indeterminate (or a spinner) when it cannot.
For long operations, show progress and, where possible, remaining time or step so users decide whether to wait.
Respect prefers-reduced-motion for animated stripes/indeterminate motion.

entries.name: Spinner / Loader
entries.summary: An animated indeterminate indicator signaling that a process is running when its duration is unknown.
entries.significance: The simplest 'working' signal, but overuse for content loading feels slower than skeletons because it hides layout.
key_facts: For content that will populate a known layout, skeleton screens are perceived as faster than a bare spinner.
Use aria-busy or a role status live region so screen readers know content is loading and when it finishes.
For very short waits (well under ~1s) avoid flashing a spinner at all; for longer waits switch to progress or messaging.
Respect prefers-reduced-motion by reducing spin speed or providing a static alternative.

sources.title: Everything you need to know about skeleton screens

entries.name: Skeleton screen
entries.summary: Low-fidelity gray placeholders shaped like the incoming content, shown while data loads to preview the forthcoming layout.
entries.significance: Improves perceived performance by shifting attention from waiting to the content taking shape; standard on content-heavy apps.
key_facts: The term was popularized by Luke Wroblewski in 2013 for reducing perceived wait time.
Users perceive skeletons as faster than spinners even when actual load time is identical, because layout is anticipated.
Skeletons should mirror the real content's structure; mismatched shapes cause a jarring swap.
Expose loading state to assistive tech (aria-busy / status) rather than announcing empty placeholder shapes.

sources.title: Everything you need to know about skeleton screens

entries.name: Carousel
entries.summary: A rotating set of slides showing one or a few items at a time, with controls to advance through the sequence.
entries.significance: Fits multiple items in limited space, but auto-rotating carousels are widely shown to have low engagement past the first slide.
key_facts: Auto-advancing carousels must offer pause/stop controls and respect prefers-reduced-motion (WCAG 2.2.2 for moving content).
Provide accessible previous/next controls and slide indicators with labels per the APG carousel pattern.
Content beyond the first slide gets little attention; put the most important content on slide one or avoid carousels for critical info.
Ensure keyboard operation and that off-screen slides are hidden from tab order/screen readers appropriately.

entries.name: Stepper / Wizard (multi-step flow)
entries.summary: A guided multi-step process that breaks a complex task into sequential steps with a visible progress indicator.
entries.significance: Reduces cognitive load for long tasks (checkout, onboarding) by chunking them and showing where the user is and what remains.
key_facts: Show the current step and total (e.g. 'Step 2 of 4') so users understand scope and progress.
Provide Back navigation and preserve entered data when moving between steps.
Validate each step before advancing, surfacing errors before the user moves on.
Distinguish a linear wizard (must complete in order) from a non-linear stepper (jump between steps).

entries.name: Rating (star rating)
entries.summary: A control (commonly stars) for capturing or displaying a score on a fixed scale, e.g. 1-5.
entries.significance: A compact way to collect subjective feedback and display aggregate quality signals in reviews and listings.
key_facts: Interactive ratings need keyboard support and an accessible value (e.g. radio group or slider semantics reporting 'X of 5 stars').
Display-only ratings must convey the value in text, not stars alone, for screen readers.
Allow clearing/changing a selection; consider half-star granularity only if the data supports it.
Star targets should meet touch-target spacing so users don't mis-tap adjacent stars.

entries.name: Segmented control
entries.summary: A linear set of two or more equal segments where selecting one switches a view or setting; a compact single-select toggle group.
entries.significance: A space-efficient way to switch between a small number of mutually exclusive views (e.g. List/Grid) with all options visible.
key_facts: Best for 2-5 mutually exclusive, equally-weighted options that fit on one line.
Implement as a single-select group (radiogroup or tablist depending on whether it switches views), with roving tabindex.
The selected segment must be visually distinct beyond color and expose selected state to assistive tech.
Each segment must meet touch-target size; avoid long labels that unbalance segments.

entries.name: Empty state
entries.summary: The screen shown when a view has no content yet (first use, no results, or cleared data), explaining why and what to do next.
entries.significance: Turns a potentially confusing blank screen into guidance, onboarding, or reassurance, and is a key first-run experience moment.
key_facts: Distinguish types: first-use (no data yet), user-cleared (e.g. empty inbox), and no-results (search/filter returned nothing).
Explain the cause and offer a clear next action (e.g. 'Add your first project') rather than a bare 'No data'.
For no-results states, suggest how to broaden or correct the query.
Keep illustrations lightweight and ensure the guidance text is accessible, not conveyed by image alone.

entries.name: Tree view
entries.summary: A hierarchical list of nested, expandable/collapsible nodes for browsing parent-child structures like file systems.
entries.significance: The standard way to present and navigate deep hierarchical data while managing complexity through collapse.
key_facts: Follows the APG tree view pattern with role tree/treeitem, aria-expanded on parent nodes, and aria-level for depth.
Arrow keys navigate and expand/collapse: Right expands, Left collapses/moves to parent, Up/Down move between visible nodes.
Indentation communicates depth visually; expose depth to assistive tech via aria-level/setsize/posinset.
Provide clear expand/collapse affordances (disclosure triangles) with adequate touch targets.
